skill.name: fixing-motion-performance
skill.description: Guides AI coding assistants to diagnose and fix UI animation performance issues without changing the existing animation stack.
skill.summary: Improve UI motion by favoring compositor-friendly animation patterns.
skill.author: ibelick

security_audit.summary: The static findings were reviewed against the Markdown content and were all false positives. The file contains guidance for animation performance reviews, with no executable code, network access, credential access, prompt injection, or malicious intent found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Slash Command Example
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The text at this line is a Markdown command example for invoking the skill, not Ruby shell backtick execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The backticks delimit inline Markdown text. No Ruby code or shell execution context is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Slash Command With File Argument
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The text at this line documents a slash command format with a file placeholder, not executable shell syntax.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is an inline Markdown usage example. There is no script, interpreter directive, or command execution behavior.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Description Misread As Weak Crypto
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The description says the skill fixes animation performance issues and does not mention cryptography or hashing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is frontmatter prose. No weak cryptographic algorithm, API call, or crypto configuration appears there.

low_findings.title: False Positive: CSS Variable Guidance Misread As Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The line advises local scoping for animated CSS variables and does not inspect the system or environment.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The wording is frontend performance guidance. It contains no filesystem, process, host, or environment enumeration.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Layer Guidance Misread As Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The line warns against too many promoted rendering layers and does not collect system information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line concerns browser compositor layers. No reconnaissance command, API, or data collection path is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: View Transition Guidance Misread As Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The line gives UI design guidance for interaction-heavy interfaces and does not probe system state.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The phrase is about browser view transitions. It contains no instruction to enumerate files, users, hosts, or processes.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Cancellation Guidance Misread As Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The line describes when to avoid view transitions and contains no system inspection behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The wording is ordinary UI interaction guidance. No code path or instruction gathers local or remote system details.

content.value_statement: Animation changes can create jank when they trigger layout, paint, or excessive browser layers. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical rules for reviewing and improving motion within the existing frontend stack.
actual_capabilities: Reviews UI animation code for layout, paint, scroll, and layer performance risks.
Recommends transform and opacity based motion when the interaction allows it.
Guides batching of DOM reads and writes to reduce forced layout work.
Suggests safer approaches for scroll-linked motion, visibility pauses, and off-screen animation.
Helps preserve the existing animation library unless the user requests migration.
limitations: It provides guidance and review criteria, not automated performance profiling.
It does not replace browser DevTools, trace analysis, or device testing.
It does not include framework-specific code samples for every animation library.
It cannot confirm runtime smoothness without access to the running application.

use_cases.title: Review a janky component
use_cases.description: Identify animation patterns that may trigger layout, paint, or excessive layer promotion.

use_cases.title: Plan a new interaction
use_cases.description: Choose motion techniques that fit the interaction while keeping browser work predictable.

use_cases.title: Set review standards
use_cases.description: Apply shared performance rules during pull request reviews for UI animation changes.

prompt_templates.title: Basic animation review
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of one component or file
prompt_templates.prompt: Use fixing-motion-performance to review this component for animation performance issues. List violations, explain why each matters, and suggest concrete fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Scroll motion check
prompt_templates.prompt: Use fixing-motion-performance to assess this scroll-linked animation. Check for scroll polling, layout work, paint-heavy effects, and off-screen animation.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor guidance
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced improvement planning for an existing interaction
prompt_templates.prompt: Use fixing-motion-performance to propose a refactor plan for this animation. Preserve the current library and prioritize transform, opacity, and batched measurement.

prompt_templates.title: Performance review standard
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced pull request or design system review
prompt_templates.prompt: Use fixing-motion-performance as a review rubric for this motion change. Rank findings by priority and include code-level alternatives for each issue.

output_examples.input: Review a card hover animation that changes width and box shadow on every pointer move.
output_examples.output: The review flags continuous layout and paint work, then recommends measuring once and animating scale or opacity instead.

output_examples.input: Assess a scroll reveal effect driven by scroll events and repeated element measurements.
output_examples.output: The review recommends IntersectionObserver or scroll timelines, batched measurements, and pausing animations when elements leave the viewport.

output_examples.input: Evaluate a pull request that adds blur animation across a full-screen overlay.
output_examples.output: The review warns that large animated blur is paint-heavy and suggests a short opacity or translate effect instead.

best_practices: Share the relevant component or animation file with the prompt.
State the target device class and interaction goal before asking for fixes.
Validate important recommendations with browser profiling tools.
anti_patterns: Do not ask it to rewrite the animation stack without an explicit migration goal.
Do not use it as a substitute for runtime performance measurement.
Do not treat every paint or layout animation as forbidden without context.

faq.question: What does this skill help fix?
faq.answer: It helps identify UI animation choices that can cause jank, unnecessary layout work, heavy paint, or excessive browser layers.

faq.question: Does it support Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Will it migrate my animation library?
faq.answer: No. The skill says to keep the existing stack unless the user explicitly requests a migration.

faq.question: Can it profile my application automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides review guidance and recommended fixes, but runtime profiling still requires browser tooling.

faq.question: Which animation properties does it prefer?
faq.answer: It generally prefers transform and opacity because they can often run on the compositor.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The audit found only false positives in Markdown guidance and no executable or malicious behavior.
